text: This is a forty-six-year-old female with chronic left-sided abdominal pain. A thorough evaluation led to a colonoscopy. This exam revealed a large submucosal mass in the proximal descending colon.
The CT scan seen here shows a six centimeter fat containing lesion. Laparoscopic exploration revealed ink in the descending colon.
The surgeon identifies inflammation in the area, using intraoperative colonoscopy to visually confirm the location of the submucosal lesion within the colon.
The laparoscopic light is turned off, and an endoscope is positioned directly at the level of the lesion. The mass is visually identified at the anti-mesenteric border of the colon.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic clip applier to mark the region of interest, followed by the placement of robotic ports along the patient's right side.
The surgeon uses robotic assistance to sweep the small bowel away from the colon, with the robot docked at the patient's left hip.
The surgeon observes the area of the lesion marked by ink and a clip. A longitudinal incision is made in the colon using a monopolar hook, revealing the underlying tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the lumen of the colon is visualized, with visible thermal spread from the instrument affecting surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a suction evacuation system to maintain clear visualization while continuing dissection along the medial border of the submucosal mass in the colon.
The assistant uses a grasper to provide traction, while the robotic instrument applies cautery to continue the dissection along the marked region of the colon.

The surgeon performs a full thickness dissection of the colon, addressing the severely inflamed mucosa overlying the lesion. The lipoma has caused a significant inflammatory response in the submucosal plane, which is visibly affected during the dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection near the lesion using a monopolar hook, maintaining close proximity to the marked area while ensuring precise tissue separation.

However, compared to an ultrasonic dissector, which could have been used laparoscopically, there was a lack of precision and a great deal of thermal spread. A scissor with cautery may have offered increased precision.
The surgeon dissects the mass away from the subserosal plane, revealing the lipoma positioned adjacent to the colon. The colotomy is held open, preparing for a transverse closure.

The surgeon gently dissects the descending colon away from the lateral sidewall to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis. Full thickness stay sutures are placed in the colon, beginning with the lateral portion, in preparation for closure.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to hold the stay suture up toward the abdominal wall. Another suture is placed in the mid portion of the colotomy and tied down, preparing for the planned stapled closure.

The surgeon secures the suture with several knots before cutting and removing the needle from the patient's abdomen. A third full thickness stitch is then placed at the medial portion of the colotomy.
The surgeon uses a robotic arm to grasp the suture and elevate it toward the abdominal wall. A robotic stapler is introduced into the abdomen for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses the medial stay suture to adjust the angle for closure. The medial portion of the colotomy is visibly closed as the robotic stapler is fired to complete the transverse closure.
The surgeon places another stay suture to approximate the two sides of the colotomy, aiming to minimize the amount of colon removed during the stapled closure.

The surgeon secures the suture with several knots and removes the needle from the abdomen. The robotic stapler is reintroduced, and with the assistance of the grasper and stay sutures, the proper angle is achieved for stapling.
The surgeon closes the stapler, which visibly cracks through the inflamed colonic tissue, likely due to the presence of a lipoma at the site.
A stay suture is placed to approximate the edges of the colotomy, preparing the tissue for closure. The surgeon considers using a thicker stapler reload for this position.
The surgeon successfully closes the colotomy using a stapler, with the inflamed colonic tissue positioned away from the stapling site, ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon uses an assistant grasper to manipulate the stay suture and anastomotic edge, achieving proper angulation of the collotomy. The stapler is then applied to safely continue the anastomosis.
The surgeon demonstrates the minimal excision of the colon, highlighting the importance of stay sutures in repositioning the colon and maintaining the anastomotic edge during the procedure.

The surgeon closes the colotomy using a stapler, ensuring the closure does not result in stenosis. The stapler is applied carefully to avoid complications, highlighting the technical difficulty of this portion of the operation.
Alternatively, the colotomy could have been sutured closed primarily. I performed this procedure at the beginning of my learning curve using the XI platform.

My comfort level with a stapled closure was certainly greater at that time compared to a sutured closure. Having gained additional experience, I would likely perform a sutured closure if performing this procedure again.

The surgeon places the lipoma and the anastomotic edge into an endocatch bag, closes the bag, and extracts the specimen from the abdomen. The operative field is then inspected for any remaining issues.
The surgeon inspects the closed staple line, noting the absence of bleeding or stenosis in the surgical site.

In reviewing this case, I believe that the scissor may have offered additional accuracy. Also, a sutured closure would likely have been faster and more cost-effective compared to the stapled closure seen in this video.
